project_identification.lead_agency: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.cross_streets: W. Greenleaf Ave. and Riverbend Parkway
project_location.within_2_miles_schools: Riverbend High School, Greenleaf Elementary School, Feather River Community Charter School

development_type.transportation_type: On-site public streets, bicycle lanes, and transit stop improvements along W. Greenleaf Ave.
development_type.other_development_type: Neighborhood park (4.0 acres), greenway and multi-use trail (approximately 1.8 miles), and a small community center (6,500 sq ft) to serve residents and employees.

project_issues_discussed_in_document.other_project_issues: Energy use and greenhouse gas emissions; environmental justice and community health; construction-related disruption to nearby residents; and climate change resiliency (urban heat island reduction, shade trees, and flood resilience).

land_use_and_project_description.present_land_use_zoning_general_plan_des:
The approximately 48.7-acre project site consists of three contiguous parcels located on the western edge of the City of Riverbend. The site is currently in active agricultural use (primarily irrigated row crops and seasonal alfalfa) with a single rural residence and several aging accessory farm structures near the northeast corner of the property.

Existing zoning: AG-20 (Agricultural, 20-acre minimum lot size) with an Urban Reserve overlay.
Existing General Plan land use designation: Urban Reserve/Agriculture. The City’s 2035 General Plan identifies the broader area as part of the West Riverbend Growth Area, anticipated for future urban development subject to adoption of a specific plan and associated infrastructure master planning.

Surrounding land uses:
- North: Single-family residential subdivision (R-1 zoning) and Greenleaf Neighborhood Park across W. Greenleaf Ave.
- South: Active orchards and row crop agriculture in unincorporated Sutter County.
- East: Riverbend High School campus and a small neighborhood commercial center at the intersection of W. Greenleaf Ave. and Riverbend Parkway.
- West: Irrigated agricultural fields extending toward the Feather River, with a levee and riparian corridor approximately 0.5 miles to the west.

The site is generally flat, with elevations ranging from approximately 45 to 52 feet above mean sea level. There are no existing on-site wetlands or streams, but an irrigation canal runs along the southern boundary and several shallow agricultural drainage swales traverse the property.
land_use_and_project_description.project_description:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project proposes to transform an approximately 48.7-acre agricultural site into a compact, walkable mixed-use neighborhood that provides new housing, employment, services, and recreational amenities within the City of Riverbend’s West Riverbend Growth Area.

Key components of the project include:

1. Residential Development (312 units total)
- A mix of housing types intended to serve a range of incomes and household sizes, including:
  • 168 single-family detached homes on small lots (3,000–4,500 sq ft lots)
  • 72 townhome units in attached 3- to 4-plex buildings
  • 72 multi-family apartments in three 3-story buildings near the village center
- Of the 72 apartments, approximately 24 units (about 8% of total units) are anticipated to be deed-restricted as affordable to low- and moderate-income households pursuant to an inclusionary housing agreement with the City.
- Residential development will be organized into distinct neighborhoods connected by a network of local streets, sidewalks, and multi-use trails, with front doors and porches oriented toward tree-lined streets and open spaces.

2. Commercial and Office Uses
- Approximately 82,000 square feet of neighborhood-serving commercial space, including a small grocery, cafés, restaurants, personal services (such as a dry cleaner and salon), and neighborhood retail (e.g., pharmacy, small hardware store).
- Approximately 65,000 square feet of office space in 2- to 3-story mixed-use and stand-alone office buildings intended for professional services, medical offices, co-working spaces, and small local businesses.
- Ground-floor commercial uses will be concentrated along a main street-style spine adjacent to a central plaza, with upper-floor office and some residential units above in selected buildings.

3. Community Facilities and Open Space
- A 4.0-acre public neighborhood park located near the center of the site, programmed with multi-use turf fields, a children’s playground, picnic areas with shade structures, a half-basketball court, and informal seating areas.
- Approximately 1.8 miles of multi-use trails and greenway corridors that connect residential neighborhoods to the park, the village center, and existing sidewalks and bike lanes on W. Greenleaf Ave. and Riverbend Parkway.
- A 6,500-square-foot community center building near the park that will include a flexible meeting room, small fitness room, and space for after-school programs and neighborhood events. The building is anticipated to be owned and operated by a homeowners’ association or community services district, in coordination with the City.
- A linear green buffer with enhanced landscaping and a Class I multi-use path along the southern edge of the site, providing a transition to adjacent agricultural uses, while maintaining necessary setback and access for existing irrigation infrastructure.

4. Transportation and Circulation Improvements
- Construction of an internal public street network that includes two primary east–west collector streets and several north–south local streets designed with traffic calming features, on-street parking, and continuous sidewalks.
- Installation of Class II bike lanes on the primary internal collectors, connecting to existing and planned bicycle facilities on W. Greenleaf Ave. and Riverbend Parkway.
- A new, signalized intersection at W. Greenleaf Ave. and the project’s main entry street (aligned with an existing driveway to Riverbend High School), subject to City and Caltrans approval.
- A dedicated transit stop with a bus pull-out, shelter, seating, and real-time arrival information along W. Greenleaf Ave., intended to support future expansion of the regional transit agency’s fixed-route service.
- Pedestrian-oriented design features including high-visibility crosswalks, curb extensions at key intersections, street trees, and pedestrian-scale lighting.

5. Infrastructure and Utilities
- Extension and upsizing of existing municipal water and sewer lines from W. Greenleaf Ave. into the project site, consistent with the City’s adopted Water and Sewer Master Plans.
- On-site stormwater management system incorporating a combination of underground piping, bio-swales, and landscaped detention basins sized to accommodate cumulative runoff from the project area and designed to meet current low impact development (LID) standards.
- Undergrounding of new electrical, telecommunications, and gas utilities within public rights-of-way.
- Installation of high-efficiency LED street lighting and dark-sky-compliant fixtures in the commercial and residential areas.

6. Entitlements Requested
- Adoption of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan establishing land use designations, development standards, design guidelines, and implementation measures for the 48.7-acre site.
- General Plan Amendment to change the land use designation from Urban Reserve/Agriculture to Mixed Use Residential/Commercial and Park/Open Space consistent with the Specific Plan.
- Rezone from AG-20 (Agricultural) to a combination of:
  • MU-V (Mixed-Use Village)
  • R-3 (Medium Density Residential)
  • OS-P (Open Space – Park)
- Approval of a Planned Unit Development (PUD) overlay to allow for flexible lotting, shared parking arrangements, and alternative development standards that support compact, walkable design while maintaining overall development intensity consistent with the General Plan.
- Tentative subdivision map(s) to create residential lots, commercial pads, public streets, park parcels, and common area lots.
- Major Use Permit(s) for mixed-use buildings exceeding two stories, the community center, and certain commercial uses (e.g., restaurants with outdoor seating) as required by the Zoning Code.
- Site Plan and Design Review for all phases of development, including architecture, landscaping, signage, and lighting.
- Approval of a Development Agreement between the City and the project applicant addressing phasing, financing and construction of backbone infrastructure, affordable housing commitments, and community benefit contributions.

7. Phasing and Construction
- The project is anticipated to be constructed in three primary phases over approximately 8–10 years, depending on market conditions and infrastructure timing.
- Phase 1 (Years 1–3): Backbone infrastructure (major internal streets, utilities, and stormwater basins), initial 120 residential units, the neighborhood park, and the first 25,000 square feet of commercial/office space.
- Phase 2 (Years 3–6): Additional 120 residential units, remaining office space, additional commercial uses, and completion of the community center.
- Phase 3 (Years 6–10): Remaining residential units, buildout of commercial pads, and final connections of the trail and greenway network.

8. Sustainability Features
- All new buildings will be designed to meet or exceed the current California Green Building Standards Code (CALGreen) and applicable Title 24 energy efficiency standards.
- The project will incorporate rooftop solar photovoltaic systems on the community center, multi-family buildings, and at least 50% of commercial roofs, subject to feasibility.
- A comprehensive tree-planting program will provide at least 15% shade coverage of paved areas at tree maturity, helping to reduce urban heat island effects.
- Electric vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure will be provided in commercial parking areas and within multi-family residential parking lots, with EV-ready conduit and panels for single-family garages.

Overall, the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is intended to create a complete neighborhood that offers housing, jobs, services, and recreation within walking and biking distance of existing schools and transit, while transitioning the site from agricultural use to urban development in a manner consistent with the City’s long-range planning goals.
